summary: I was born in Vientiane, Laos in 1960. Growing up there, I lived with my grandma after my parents separated when I was five. Vientiane was where my early life and family struggles began before the turmoil of the Vietnam War affected us.

Laos was my home country where I grew up and faced many challenges. After high school, I worked in different states within Laos during the final years of the Vietnam War. The dangers left behind by the war affected my village deeply, and I was eventually kicked out rather than taken away like others.

The Vietnam War was ending around the time I was finishing high school, but its dangers lingered in Laos. My village warned young men not to fight back or they would be imprisoned or taken away, never to return. This war's aftermath forced me to flee and seek refuge across the Mekong River.

Thailand was the only place I knew of that had a refugee camp where I hoped to find safety. Crossing the Mekong River into Thailand was terrifying, especially since many people were shot while trying to cross. I stayed in a refugee camp in Thailand for two years and worked fixing cars to support myself.

The Mekong River was the dangerous barrier between Laos and the refugee camp in Thailand. I swam a mile across it against strong currents, pulling two friends behind me with a plastic wrap line. Crossing that river was a life-or-death moment for me and many others trying to escape.

The United States of America became my new home after I was sponsored to come here from the refugee camp. I first arrived in California, then moved to Michigan to meet my sponsor, but ended up in Minnesota with my cousin. America was where I learned English, worked hard, and eventually became a citizen.

California was the first place I landed when I arrived in America. I stayed in a camp there before being moved to Michigan to meet my sponsor. It was the beginning of my new life in the United States, although I didn’t stay long before heading north.

Michigan was where my sponsor lived, the person who helped me come to America. Although I never met her or remembered her name, she was the reason I could start a new life here. Before I could meet her, my cousin took me to Minnesota, but Michigan was an important step in my journey.

Minnesota became my long-term home after arriving in America. My cousin brought me here before I met my sponsor, and I lived in a small house with nine relatives. I worked various low-paying jobs, taught fighting skills in the park, and eventually settled down with my family here.

Waymar was the company where I found better work after many years of low-paying jobs. I worked there for 27 years, which gave me stability and the means to support my family. It was an important part of my American life and career.

The Corvette was one of the sports cars I bought and really liked. However, I had to sell it after having kids because it wasn’t practical for fitting car seats in the back. It was a symbol of my success before family responsibilities took priority.

Prior Lake, Minnesota is where I live now after many years of moving and working hard. It’s my home with my current wife Praiya and family. This place represents the stability and new life I built after coming to America.
